facts.fact: Context switching overhead on modern x86_64/Linux systems is approximately 1.2-1.5 microseconds per direct context switch under light load, with kernel context switches taking 1-10 microseconds
CPU scheduling overhead in densely packed Linux clusters rises to approximately 20% of total CPU time, with average context switches of 48 microseconds under high load conditions
A TLB miss can cost 1000-2000 CPU cycles or more, while L1/L2 cache misses cost 10-40 cycles; TLB misses are more expensive due to page walk requiring multiple memory accesses

When CPU switches between processes, TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) must be flushed, forcing 1000-2000 CPU cycles of overhead; thread switches within same process avoid this because threads share same address space
With 99% TLB hit rate, effective memory access time is 1.49 ns; with 95% hit rate it becomes 3.45 ns (2.3x slower); with 90% hit rate it becomes 5.9 ns (4x slower)
Default Linux file descriptor limit is 1,024 per process, but Kafka brokers should be set to at least 100,000 to track log segments and connections across many partitions
Kubernetes sends SIGTERM to containers and waits 30 seconds (default grace period) before sending SIGKILL; if containers don't handle SIGTERM, they get forcibly terminated
SIGKILL cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored by a process; SIGTERM allows the process to perform cleanup (commit WALs, close connections, free resources) before terminating
Async I/O with 100 concurrent requests completes in under 0.6 seconds with CPU usage never exceeding 15% and flat memory; thread-per-request model causes memory to spike as threads block on I/O
Go's goroutine scheduler uses M:N model (many goroutines onto N OS threads); goroutines start with 2KB stack and grow dynamically; applications can run 100,000+ goroutines efficiently
Go's scheduler implements work-stealing algorithm: when a processor's run queue is empty, it can steal goroutines from other processors' queues to keep CPU cores busy
Go 1.14+ grants each goroutine 10ms time slice; if exceeded without yielding, runtime issues forceful preemption via SIGURG signal to OS thread

Java 21 virtual threads achieved 117,448 requests per second with 844 microsecond average latency; platform threads showed 448ms latency vs virtual threads 319ms for I/O-intensive workloads (28.8% improvement)
Netflix's concurrency limit formula: concurrency = average service time × average service rate (Little's Law); Netflix eliminates manual limit tuning via adaptive concurrency limits
Modern processors (ARMv7+, ARM64, X86-64) support ASID (Address Space Identifier) or PCID (Process Context ID) to reduce TLB flush overhead on context switches
A poorly designed application can see 10x performance degradation from TLB misses alone; huge pages (2MB or 1GB) reduce TLB entries needed by 512x or more
CPU affinity (pinning processes to specific cores) effectively minimizes context switch overhead and enhances cache utilization
Thread context switches are 2-5x faster than process switches because TLB remains valid, CPU cache stays hot, and OS only saves/restores register values and stack pointers
Each OS thread needs ~8MB of stack memory; goroutines need only kilobytes; this enables 100,000+ concurrent goroutines on modest hardware
Kubernetes preStop hook runs before SIGTERM signal, allowing applications to stop accepting traffic before shutdown signal arrives
Kubernetes sends SIGTERM only to process with PID 1; if application is not running as PID 1, it will not receive graceful shutdown signal

examples.example: Netflix reduced cascading failures by implementing adaptive concurrency limits; system calculates optimal concurrency using Little's Law rather than static configuration
examples.metric: Eliminated manual concurrency tuning across microservices

examples.example: Netflix encountered timeout and hung instances when adopting Java 21 virtual threads due to blocking operations and OS thread starvation
Kafka broker performance degrades when file descriptor limit is 1,024 (Linux default); setting to 100,000+ required for production deployments with many partitions
examples.impact: Inability to open log segments or accept connections

examples.example: Go application with 10,000 concurrent connections uses work-stealing scheduler to multiplex goroutines onto 4 CPU cores; idle goroutines yield immediately via preemption signal
examples.benchmark: 10,000 goroutines on 4 cores vs equivalent thread-per-connection requiring 10,000 OS threads (~80GB memory)

examples.example: Async refactor of I/O-bound application became 6x slower when 800 tasks competed for 32 cores; 60% of CPU time spent on context switching overhead
examples.lesson: Async has overhead; only beneficial when I/O latency >> context switch cost

examples.example: Java application using virtual threads on high-concurrency I/O workload: 8898.3 req/sec vs platform threads 5410.1 req/sec; latency improved from 448ms to 319ms (28.8%)

analogies.analogy: TLB flush on process switch is like a chef losing all tools from memory when reassigned to a new kitchen; thread switch within same process is like keeping the same kitchen and just handing tools to a new chef
analogies.teaches: Why process switches are more expensive than thread switches

analogies.analogy: Virtual memory address translation is like a postal address system: virtual address is the house address you write on the envelope, page table is the postal service's internal routing, physical address is the actual house number. Multiple people (processes) can write the same house address but it routes to different physical houses
Goroutines are like book markers in a single book (OS thread): you can have thousands of markers without creating new books; OS threads are like owning separate physical books (8MB each)

Async I/O is like a restaurant using a single waiter who notes orders and checks on tables only when needed; thread-per-request is like hiring a separate waiter for each customer and having them stand idle while food cooks
analogies.teaches: Why async outperforms threads under high concurrency

analogies.analogy: SIGTERM is like politely asking someone to leave a room; SIGKILL is like forcibly carrying them out; graceful shutdown gives them time to put on shoes and grab keys
analogies.teaches: Signal handling and clean resource release

analogies.analogy: CPU cache after context switch is like a cashier who suddenly has to use a different register: all memorized prices and frequent customer names are lost, requiring slow lookups from the manual
analogies.teaches: Hidden cost of context switching beyond cycle count

common_misconceptions.misconception: Context switching is just a few microseconds, so it doesn't matter
common_misconceptions.truth: Direct cost is 1-2 microseconds, but indirect costs (cache eviction, TLB flush, pipeline flush) add 100-1000x overhead; at high context switch rates (48 μs average under load), context switching becomes 20% of CPU time

common_misconceptions.misconception: Thread and async are interchangeable concurrency models
common_misconceptions.truth: Threading context switches can occur anywhere; async switches happen only at explicit await points. Different scheduling models with different latency/throughput tradeoffs
Async adds overhead (event loop management, coroutine bookkeeping); only beneficial when I/O latency >> context switch overhead. Async refactor can be 6x slower when tasks compete for limited cores

common_misconceptions.misconception: Process isolation costs nothing because it's just page tables
common_misconceptions.truth: Process switches require TLB flush (1000-2000 CPU cycles), invalidate caches, and may require expensive shootdown synchronization; thread switches are 2-5x faster

common_misconceptions.misconception: File descriptor limits don't matter for modern applications
common_misconceptions.truth: Default 1,024 limit causes 'Too many open files' errors in Kafka, databases, and web servers at moderate concurrency; must be set to 100,000+ in production

common_misconceptions.misconception: SIGKILL is always preferred for shutdown because it's faster
common_misconceptions.truth: SIGKILL cannot be caught; application can't commit data, close connections, or release resources. SIGTERM allows graceful cleanup; forced termination causes data loss

common_misconceptions.misconception: Virtual threads eliminate the need for understanding real concurrency
common_misconceptions.truth: Virtual threads can get pinned (blocked) on non-unmountable operations (file I/O, JNI calls, synchronized blocks); thread scheduling principles still apply

common_misconceptions.misconception: More threads always equals more throughput
common_misconceptions.truth: Excessive threading causes context switching overhead to exceed work time; optimal thread count depends on blocking I/O characteristics, not arbitrary scaling

common_misconceptions.misconception: TLB misses are rare and don't significantly impact performance
common_misconceptions.truth: A 5% drop in TLB hit rate (99% → 95%) causes 2.3x slower memory access; 10% drop causes 4x slower access; poorly designed apps see 10x degradation

common_misconceptions.misconception: Goroutines are 'magic' concurrency that scales without limits
common_misconceptions.truth: Goroutines still depend on OS scheduler; at very high concurrency with CPU contention, scheduling overhead increases. Work-stealing prevents starvation but doesn't eliminate scheduling cost
